text: Like, they went & removed that AI "art". But people actually actively made notice of that. I didn't even know about the generative ai announcer until watching a YouTube video because no one confronted me about it. I'm not touching anything Capcom besides maybe second hand.

It's the opposite in my field. Coders know that generative AI can't make art but that coding agents can do the majority (by billable hour) of what we have done historically. This continual forced evolution is both exciting and tiring.

I think it was good and vital this was brought up and aired out. The fandom has to make it clear the use of generative AI is entirely unacceptable. 

The artist had a chance to prove themselves and hopefully the art team understands not to dabble.

That said I think we need to keep an eye out.

Weird world we live in that this is something I feel the need to preempt but I've been working on some surreal gloopy artwork for some tunes, so just wanted to say that I don't use generative AI in any capacity for any of my music or art projects

I rarely comment on stuff like this, but this time it might be important:  

I will never use generative AI in my games!  

The way AI works would be reason enough already btw: 
It can never allow me the level of interactive communication with my art, that I need.

Guys, do not confuse generative AI (ChatGPT and the like) with machine learning. Machine learning for local use on a specific data set is a perfectly valid application of AI. 
This is not the same as the plagiarising, resource hungry monster that's killing art.

it gets under my skin because the community has been using the term "generative art" to mean something very specific for decades, and it took only a few years of the slop deluge for the term to be corrupted beyond all meaning. i hate that we're the ones who have to move on, but such is life

my reason for going with "procedural" rather than some other term is that it's the key distinction between traditional generative art and the AI generated stuff. for any piece i create i can state precisely what the procedure was that created it, because i designed the procedure myself

i think the time has come for me to give up on trying to use the term "generative art" to describe what i do. it makes me sad, but the term has now been so thoroughly overrun by AI interlopers that it's time to admit it's unsalvageable. so, "procedural art" it is from now on, i think

My dad, having spent his life developing and teaching artistic process for photography, stone lithography, etc, sent me to engineering school with an explicit request that I learn how to create algorithms for generative art. He viewed it as the ultimate expression of his thesis on aesthetic realism.

Generative "AI" is a threat across the board, not because it could someday hypothetically be used to make something actually good, but because execs see it as free labor and have NEVER cared about quality. They don't know what art is, only how much they can sell it for.

Given what I learned about collaborators the past few months: I would not put it past people to sneak in generative art just to have a beef with a specific gamedev.

Clair Oscur has made me doubtful of certain peoples claims.

Im going to solo it.
